title: Designing Drones for Africa
gist: An interview with Maxwell Maduka, co-founder of African defense-tech firm Terra Industries, on why imported Turkish and Chinese drones fail in African conditions, how Terra engineers around heat/dust/humidity with its own ArtemisOS autonomy stack, and its strategy to become the defense prime for the Global South.

themes.theme: Why imported drones fail in Africa

insights.claim: Cheap Turkish and Chinese drones are hard to sustain locally, are designed for other terrains, and leave operators unable to audit firmware, creating espionage and sovereignty risks over software and data.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Maduka
insights.so_what: Defines the market gap Terra exploits: sovereignty and serviceability, not just price, in African defense procurement.
insights.quote: If your intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance backbone is still linked to these countries, and you can’t audit the firmware, your operational picture is probably insecure.
insights.locator: There’s also the worry of espionage

insights.claim: African drone design is almost the inverse of Ukraine's problem, with the 'big four' challenges being heat, dust, humidity, and topography, and heat being the trickiest because it degrades batteries and cooks the onboard compute.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Maduka
insights.so_what: The environment-specific engineering moat that imported generic kits cannot easily match.
insights.quote: It’s almost the inverse of what you’re solving for in Ukraine.
insights.locator: The big four for us are heat, dust, humidity

insights.claim: Terra's ArtemisOS software stack keeps autonomy and data under operator control with a strict human-in-the-loop policy, and the company refuses to build autonomous lethality.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Maduka
insights.so_what: Differentiates Terra on data sovereignty and ethics, a selling point to governments wary of foreign-controlled systems.
insights.quote: Nothing in our engineering or documentation stack allows a system to decide to engage on its own.
insights.locator: But we have a firm principle

insights.claim: Terra deliberately designs for defensive/awareness use cases (surveillance, search and rescue, agriculture, IED detection) because that demand is bigger and more sustainable than kinetic effects, though it assumes systems will be used for defensive kinetic purposes.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Maduka
insights.so_what: Reveals the commercial logic: persistent civilian/dual-use demand over conventional warfare niches.
insights.quote: that’s where the persistent demand is, which offers a bigger and more sustainable market than kinetic effects.
insights.locator: We designed around awareness and protection use cases

insights.claim: Non-state actors have carried out over 100 drone strikes across the Sahel since 2023, making the counter-unmanned-aerial-system mission important for Terra.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Maduka
insights.so_what: Quantifies the threat driving demand for counter-drone systems in Africa.
insights.quote: since 2023, over 100 drone strikes have been carried out by terrorist organizations across the region
insights.locator: And non-state actors have been using drones

insights.claim: Terra aims to become 'the defense prime for the Global South,' avoiding direct competition with established U.S./European primes and instead serving South Asia and South America with affordable, sovereign systems.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Maduka
insights.so_what: Stakes out a defensible market positioning in underserved regions rather than contesting mature Western markets.
insights.quote: We’d like to become the defense prime for the Global South.
insights.locator: Yet in our view, the most persistent security challenges

insights.claim: Terra is building a manufacturing facility in Ghana and has a joint venture with the Defence Industries Corporation of Nigeria for local production with technology transfer, to grow a local supplier base.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Maduka
insights.so_what: Concrete localization moves that tighten Terra's feedback loop and build trust with African customers.
insights.locator: That’s why our partnership with the Defence Industries Corporation of Nigeria

insights.claim: The African defense-tech ecosystem needs four things to self-sustain (patient mission-aware capital, a local supplier base, test/certification infrastructure, and systems-engineering talent), with the supplier base being the biggest current gap.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Maduka
insights.so_what: A checklist for investors and African governments on where capital and policy can unlock the sector.
insights.quote: the supplier base is the biggest deal right now. Many critical subsystem parts are still import-dependent.
